text: The robotic setup for the right upper lobectomy includes Maryland forceps with bipolar energy, Cadea forceps, and a thoracic grasper used to position the lung. Two cigar-shaped instruments are visible in the chest cavity.

The surgeon begins the right upper lobectomy by dissecting along the bronchus intermedius to access the station seven lymph node. A small bronchial artery is addressed, facilitating the opening of the subcarinal space for thorough dissection of the station seven subcarinal lymph node. The Maryland bipolar forceps are used to achieve complete dissection efficiently.
The surgeon carefully peels the station seven lymph node from behind the bronchus intermedius, aiming to remove the lymph node whole to minimize bleeding and ensure a thorough lymphadenectomy.

The robotic arms are utilized to lift and retract while dissecting the station seven lymph node from the pericardium. The oesophagus is visible to the left, and the surgeon carefully peels the lymph node from the oesophagus, progressing towards the carina. Small vessels from the oesophagus and pericardium may be encountered during this dissection.

The surgeon uses bipolar Maryland forceps to address adhesions between the lung and the chest wall, ensuring careful manipulation to avoid tissue invasion. The forceps may cause slight charring during the procedure.

The bronchus intermedius leading to the right upper lobe bronchus is clearly visible, with the azygos vein seen in the background. The surgeon identifies and prepares to remove a station R-ten hilar lymph node to facilitate the passage of a stapler around the back of the right upper lobe.

The surgeon uses Maryland bipolar forceps to carefully dissect and remove lymph nodes whole, minimizing bleeding and ensuring a complete lymphadenectomy. The right upper lobe is visible to the right of the forceps, with the bronchus intermedius located below the right upper lobe bronchus. Above the right upper lobe bronchus is the right main bronchus, which passes under the azygos vein to join the left main bronchus and form the trachea.
The surgeon carefully removes the station ten lymph node, ensuring all blood supply and small lymphatic vessels are addressed to extract the node intact.

The surgical team utilizes the Cefolio method for port placement, inserting a 5mm port posteriorly, two 8mm ports in the eighth intercostal space, with the camera positioned centrally. An assistant port is placed anteriorly in the ninth or tenth intercostal space.

The surgeon carefully dissects the station R-eleven lymph node located between the right upper lobe and the bronchus intermedius. The pulmonary artery becomes visible to the right of the lymph node during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully removes a lymph node during the right upper lobectomy, providing clear access around the right upper lobe bronchus. This maneuver enhances visibility of the pulmonary artery and potentially the posterior descending segmental vessel.

The surgeon identifies the station R-Eleven S lymph node, located interlobar between the upper, lower, and middle lobes of the lung. This node is positioned superiorly, with the inferior counterpart situated between the middle and lower lobes.

The surgeon ensures complete removal of the lymph node before moving to the fissure between the upper and middle lobes. Adhesions are visible, and the surgeon carefully dissects them to access the fissure, which provides a clear view of the pulmonary artery.

The camera view reveals the confluence of the fissures, with the upper lobe visible above, the middle lobe to the right, and the lower lobe at the bottom left. The pulmonary artery is typically seen in this anatomical region.

The surgeon retracts the upper lobe downward, revealing the azygos vein above the surgical field. The station ten lymph node is being removed, with the right main bronchus and superior vena cava (SVC) visible anteriorly.
The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy of station R2 and R4 above the azygos vein. The posterior limits of the vagus nerve are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon removes the station R-IV lymph node, ensuring complete excision from beneath the ozygous vein. The superior vena cava (SVC) is visible anteriorly, and the trachea and vagus nerve are exposed posteriorly following the lymphadenectomy. The surgical field is cleared of fatty tissue for a thorough lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon retracts the upper lobe posteriorly to identify the upper lobe vein. Careful dissection is performed around the vein to avoid damaging the pulmonary artery, which is located just behind the heart.

The surgeon has successfully navigated around the vein and is preparing to use a Tan-Forty-Five stapler with a leader catheter. The soft leader catheter is positioned to facilitate effective negotiation of the vessels, providing guidance for the assistant.
The surgeon rotates the Signia powered stapler to align with the vein, and the stapler is fired smoothly, securing the vessel.

The surgeon returns to the right upper lobe bronchus, which is clearly visible alongside the right upper lobe and bronchus intermedius. The pulmonary artery is also distinctly seen, facilitated by the removal of lymph nodes from station XI and station X. A leader catheter is used to aid in negotiating the vessel, with the option of using a sling for additional support.

The surgeon uses a purple 45 gold tip tri-stapler to divide the bronchus intermedius, which is clearly visible in the surgical field. Following the division, lymph nodes located behind the bronchus are being excised, revealing the truncal branch.
The surgeon identifies and manipulates L-ten hilar lymph nodes located near the pulmonary structures.

The surgeon has moved to the front to identify and navigate around the vessels. The pulmonary artery is exposed, revealing two truncal branches: anterior and apical. The bronchus has already been taken, ensuring a safe method for firing the vessel using a Tan-Forty-Five stapler with a leader cath. The assistant operates from a low and anterior port, facilitating a straightforward approach without reticulation.

The surgeon completes the pulmonary lobectomy by addressing the fissure using a purple sixty stapler, creating straight staple lines to minimize air leaks. The final two firings are performed to secure the fissure, followed by the removal of the lobe using an EWP.
The surgeon uses a strong cotton bag to remove the excised right upper lobe through the assistant port, completing the right upper lobectomy procedure.
